summary: Gruber explains why he's abandoning his clever Unicode domain name (✪df.ws) for his Twitter URL shortener in favor of a plain ASCII alternative (df4.us). He originally registered the Internationalized Domain Name because it looked sharp, but discovered widespread software incompatibility — Twitter clients, web browsers including Firefox, and even AT&T's 3G DNS servers couldn't properly handle non-ASCII characters in URLs. Despite being stubbornly defiant about the issue for over a year, the problems never improved, forcing him to concede defeat.
key_points: Internationalized Domain Names allow Unicode characters via Punycode encoding, but real-world support is deeply lacking
A wide range of software — Twitter clients (especially Adobe AIR apps), Firefox, and even Twitter.com itself — failed to properly parse non-ASCII URLs
AT&T's DNS servers for 3G connections couldn't resolve IDN domains, breaking links even in compliant clients
Gruber held out for over a year hoping the ecosystem would catch up, but nothing improved
The switch to df4.us represents a pragmatic concession of aesthetics to functionality
money_quotes: Because it seemed fun and looked sharp, I registered the domain name ✪df.ws.
What I didn't foresee was the tremendous amount of software out there that does not properly parse non-ASCII characters in URLs, particularly IDN domain names.
I was stubbornly defiant about this — particularly with regard to Twitter clients that didn't parse these URLs correctly.
But the problems never got better.
Actually using an IDN domain has, as outlined above, proven to be an enormous pain in the ass, but registering and managing the settings for the domain itself, at Dynadot, has been no problem at all.
key_insight: A clever, aesthetically pleasing technical choice proved unworkable because the surrounding ecosystem — browsers, clients, DNS servers — never caught up with the standard it depended on.
